mappings.rationaleAI: Homo sapiens covering behavioral capacities shared with primates (A.1.a) and distinctive to humans including language and culture (A.1.b), genetic and environmental factors (A.2), and life cycle stages (B).
Emotions as neurophysiologically grounded states constituting the affective dimension of behaviour and experience (A.3.c), including the sentiments and the pleasant and unpleasant.
The mind encompassing cognitive, conative, and affective dimensions (A.3), with the mind-body problem and attribution of mind to artificial systems relevant to emergent problems (A.5).
Childhood covering ages one to twelve as a stage in the development of human behaviour (B.5).
Sexual orientation influenced by genetic factors as elements of genetic endowment (A.2.a) and prenatal hormonal environment as environmental conditions (A.2.b).
Young adulthood and maturity as a stage in the development of human behaviour (B.7).
Adolescence from puberty to adulthood as a stage in the development of human behaviour (B.6).
Infancy covering the first 18 months as a stage in the development of human behaviour (B.4).
Reasoning as a cognitive capacity encompassing deductive, inductive, and abductive processing within the cognitive dimension of behaviour (A.3.a).

Learning as the acquisition of knowledge and skills relevant to memory and concept formation in the cognitive dimension (A.3.a) and observed elements of behaviour reflecting habits and capacities (A.4).
Belief as a subjective mental attitude formed through perception and inference, relevant to concept formation, ideation, and reasoning in the cognitive dimension (A.3.a).
Fear as an unpleasant emotional state with amygdala-mediated arousal, relevant to the affective dimension of behaviour including the emotions and the unpleasant (A.3.c).
Cognitive processes including judging, reasoning, concept formation, and problem-solving within the cognitive dimension of behaviour (A.3.a).
AI as the comparison of human and artificial machine-created intelligence, an emergent problem in the study of human behaviour (A.5).
Cumulative cultural transmission as a distinctive human behavioral capacity (A.1.b) and cultural conditioning as an environmental condition shaping behaviour (A.2.b).
Psychology as the scientific study of mind and behaviour spanning cognitive, affective, and conative dimensions (A.3) and general aspects of human development (B.1).
Play as an activity supporting cognitive and social development during early childhood and childhood (B.5).
The full range of human behavioral responses shaped by genetic, environmental, and cultural factors (A) and observed elements of behaviour including actions, habits, and capacities (A.4).
Personality as behavioral, cognitive, and emotional patterns reflecting persisting dispositional tendencies, habits, and capacities (A.4).
Primates sharing behavioral capacities with humans (A.1.a) and the evolutionary development of mankind through phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c).
Animals providing the comparative framework for behavioral capacities humans share with other primates and higher mammals (A.1.a).
Darwin's work on human evolution and The Expression of the Emotions relevant to the evolutionary development of human traits through phylogenetic continuity (A.1.c).
Human sexuality encompassing desire and attraction as drives and needs within the conative dimension of behaviour (A.3.b).
Gender as social, psychological, and cultural dimensions shaped by cultural conditioning and personal socialization as environmental conditions (A.2.b).
Anthropology studying humanity across biology, society, and culture, relevant to mankind's relationship to nature (A.1) and the nature-nurture controversy (A.2).
Intelligence as cognitive capacity for reasoning and problem-solving (A.3.a) and the comparison of human and artificial intelligence (A.5).
Freud's psychoanalytic theory addressing the unconscious, drives, and libido within the conative dimension (A.3.b) and emotions and the sentiments within the affective dimension (A.3.c).
Oral tradition as a medium of cumulative cultural transmission, a behavioral capacity held to be distinctive of humans (A.1.b).
